---
title: "Exercise: Count A, C, G, T, and other"
format:
  html:
    toc: true
    code-copy: true
    code-overflow: wrap
    highlight-style: github
---

## Goal

Write a function

```rust
fn base_counts(seq: &[u8]) -> (usize, usize, usize, usize, usize)
```

that returns `(a, c, g, t, other)` — the number of A, C, G, T, and "anything else" bases in a DNA sequence, treating upper and lower case as the same letter. "Other" counts every byte that is not A/C/G/T: `N`, ambiguity codes like `R`/`Y`/`S`, gap characters, and any garbage.

## Background

Per-base composition is the first thing every QC tool reports (FastQC, seqkit, NanoStat, ...). You are building a tiny version. By the end of the week your tool will handle gigabyte FASTQ files in seconds; for now we feed it a string on the command line.

## A note on tuples

This is the first exercise that returns more than one value. Rust has [tuples](https://doc.rust-lang.org/book/ch03-02-data-types.html#the-tuple-type) for bundling several values of (possibly different) types into one return value:

```rust
let (a, c, g, t, other) = base_counts(seq);
```

The pattern `(a, c, g, t, other)` on the left is [destructuring](https://doc.rust-lang.org/book/ch18-03-pattern-syntax.html#destructuring-to-break-apart-values) — it pulls the five fields apart into separate local variables in one step.

On day 3 we will replace the bare tuple with a proper `BaseCounts` struct so the fields have names. For now, the position-based tuple is enough.

## The exercise

```bash
cd day1/ex-base-counts
```

Make all six tests pass without changing the signature, `main`, or the tests.

## How to run

```bash
cargo test
cargo run -- ACGTacgtNNX
```

Expected output for `ACGTacgtNNX`:

```
A: 2
C: 2
G: 2
T: 2
other: 3
```

## Hints

::: {.callout-tip collapse="true" title="Hint 1 — five mutable counters"}
You need five separate counters. Declare each with [`let mut`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/book/ch03-01-variables-and-mutability.html), typed as [`usize`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/std/primitive.usize.html) (the natural integer type for counts and array indices):

```rust
let mut a: usize = 0;
let mut c: usize = 0;
// ...
```
:::

::: {.callout-tip collapse="true" title="Hint 2 — match with alternative patterns"}
You can combine alternatives in a single [`match`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/book/ch06-02-match.html) arm with `|`:

```rust
match b {
    b'A' | b'a' => a += 1,
    // ...
    _ => other += 1,
}
```

`_` is the [wildcard pattern](https://doc.rust-lang.org/book/ch06-02-match.html#catch-all-patterns-and-the-_-placeholder) — it matches anything you have not already handled. Use it for the "other" arm.
:::

::: {.callout-tip collapse="true" title="Hint 3 — returning the five counts"}
At the end of the function, write the tuple as the last expression (no `;`):

```rust
(a, c, g, t, other)
```

That is the function's return value.
:::

## Solution

::: {.callout-note collapse="true" title="Solution"}
```rust
fn base_counts(seq: &[u8]) -> (usize, usize, usize, usize, usize) {
    let mut a: usize = 0;
    let mut c: usize = 0;
    let mut g: usize = 0;
    let mut t: usize = 0;
    let mut other: usize = 0;
    for &b in seq {
        match b {
            b'A' | b'a' => a += 1,
            b'C' | b'c' => c += 1,
            b'G' | b'g' => g += 1,
            b'T' | b't' => t += 1,
            _ => other += 1,
        }
    }
    (a, c, g, t, other)
}
```

Full reference implementation in `day1/ex-base-counts-solution/src/main.rs`.
:::

## Where this leads

- **Day 3**: replace the five-tuple return with a named `BaseCounts` struct, rewrite the loop as an iterator [`fold`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/std/iter/trait.Iterator.html#method.fold), and decide formally how to treat IUPAC ambiguity codes (R, Y, S, W, K, M, ...).
- **Day 4**: apply this to every record of a real `.fastq.gz` streamed through [`noodles-fastq`](https://docs.rs/noodles-fastq/latest/noodles_fastq/).
